title: Design and Analysis of Algorithms
description: Advanced study of algorithm design paradigms and complexity analysis. This course extends the algorithmic foundations from CS201 to cover divide-and-conquer, greedy algorithms, dynamic programming, network flow, and introduction to NP-completeness. Students learn to prove algorithm correctness, analyze worst-case and average-case complexity, and select appropriate algorithmic strategies for different problem types. The course includes significant theoretical content including amortized analysis, randomized algorithms, and computational complexity classes. Both CS201 (for data structures proficiency) and CS202 (for proof techniques and mathematical maturity) are required prerequisites. Students will complete algorithm design projects and written proof assignments throughout the semester.
topics: Divide and conquer: master theorem, Strassen's algorithm
Greedy algorithms: activity selection, Huffman coding
Dynamic programming: knapsack, sequence alignment, shortest paths
Graph algorithms: MST (Prim, Kruskal), network flow
outcomes: Design algorithms using divide-and-conquer, greedy, and DP paradigms
Prove algorithm correctness using loop invariants and induction
Analyze algorithm complexity using recurrences and amortized analysis
Identify NP-complete problems and apply reductions
Apply network flow algorithms to optimization problems
Select appropriate algorithmic paradigms for given problem types
